title: Higher coherent cohomology in weight one
speaker: Si-Ying Lee
abstract: I will talk about a proof of local-global compatibility at p for higher coherent cohomology mod p in weight one for Hilbert modular varieties at an unramified prime, assuming we are not in middle degree. I will discuss some key ingredients to the proof, including a geometric Jacquet-Langlands transfer map to suitable quaternionic Shimura varieties, and a doubling map in this setting.

proof_stages.label: Weight-one Serre motivation over Q
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from the weight-one mod-p Serre philosophy for modular curves: weight-one modular forms correspond to odd two-dimensional mod-p Galois representations unramified at p.

proof_stages.label: Hilbert modular weight-one cohomology setup
proof_stages.summary: The setting is generalized to a totally real field with p split, a good-reduction toroidal compactification of the Hilbert modular variety, its Hodge line bundles, and the parallel weight-one automorphic sheaf, with emphasis that higher coherent cohomology in this weight can remain nonzero after non-Eisenstein localization.

proof_stages.label: Expected Serre-type statement and local-global theorem
proof_stages.summary: The expected analogue says that odd mod-p Galois representations unramified at p should appear in every relevant cohomological degree, and the proved direction is a local-global compatibility theorem: any non-Eisenstein Hecke eigensystem in weight-one coherent cohomology has associated Galois representation unramified at p.

proof_stages.label: Degree-zero doubling mechanism
proof_stages.summary: Reviewing the modular-curve H^0 case, multiplication by the Hasse invariant and its U_p translate give a doubling map into weight p ordinary cohomology, and the relation U_p^2-T_pU_p=0 on its image combines with ordinary local-global compatibility to force unramifiedness at p.

proof_stages.label: Higher ordinary transfer to quaternionic Shimura varieties
proof_stages.summary: Boxer-Pilloni higher Hida theory supplies U_p operators in higher coherent cohomology and a geometric Jacquet-Langlands transfer, identifying ordinary regular-weight H^i classes on the Hilbert modular variety with H^0 classes on a quaternionic Shimura variety ramified at a prescribed set of real places.

proof_stages.label: Weight-one transfer and higher doubling proposition
proof_stages.summary: Weyl-group considerations suggest a regular weight with i entries 2-p and the rest p, and although the direct in-degree weight-raising map is unavailable, the speaker constructs a direct weight-one transfer to the quaternionic H^0 target whose doubling with U_p is injective, ordinary, and satisfies the needed U_p/T_p relation.

proof_stages.label: Construction from Gamma_0(p) strata and Hecke correspondences
proof_stages.summary: The transfer is built from the special fiber of the Hilbert modular variety with Gamma_0(p) level, its Goren-Oort strata, and the component of the T_p correspondence supported on the desired stratum, which factors through lower-dimensional/quaternionic Shimura varieties and explains the relation between T_p and U_p.

proof_stages.label: Middle-degree exception and duality range
proof_stages.summary: In response to a question, the speaker explains that the argument proves the key relation only away from the middle degree I=d/2, where the target becomes a quaternionic Shimura set and the map can vanish, while degrees above d/2 are handled by duality.

examples.name: Hilbert modular surface with one Goren-Oort index
examples.note: For d=2 and I={1}, the speaker describes the supersingular locus as two transverse families of projective lines and identifies the relevant target as their intersection; later this same case reveals the middle-degree obstruction, where the quaternionic Shimura variety is a Shimura set and the constructed map can be zero.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker specializes to d=2 with I={1}, identifies the images as the two supersingular/Goren-Oort components x1 and x2, describes their P^1-union geometry and transverse intersections, and traces the resulting cohomological correspondence/transfer map through this case.

passes.reason: For d=2 and one Goren-Oort index the associated quaternionic Shimura variety is the middle-boundary, zero-dimensional Shimura set, and the speaker notes this case makes the transfer map zero and obstructs injectivity.

For d=2 and one Goren-Oort index, |I|=d/2, so the associated quaternionic Shimura target collapses to a 0-dimensional Shimura set and is cited as the middle-degree obstruction where the constructed map becomes zero.
It is the critical middle case d=2 with |I|=d/2, used as the bad excluded case where the desired injectivity/relation fails.

For d=2 and |I|=1=d/2 it is the boundary middle Goren–Oort case whose quaternionic target collapses to a zero-dimensional Shimura set, and it is used as the bad case where the transfer/injectivity argument fails.

For d=2 with one Goren-Oort index one is in the critical middle case I=d/2, and the speaker identifies it as the bad case where the associated quaternionic Shimura variety is a Shimura set and the constructed map can be zero, obstructing injectivity.

For d=2 with |I|=1 it is the critical middle case I=d/2, where the associated quaternionic Shimura target collapses to a 0-dimensional Shimura set and is cited as the bad case in which the transfer map becomes zero/injectivity fails.
It is the critical middle case d=2 with one index |I|=d/2, used to show the transfer/injectivity argument fails there.

In the transcript the d=2, |I|=1 middle-degree Hilbert modular surface case becomes a zero-dimensional quaternionic Shimura set and is cited as the bad case where the transfer map is zero/injectivity fails, i.e. an obstruction to the naive argument.

The d=2, |I|=1 Goren–Oort case is the critical middle-degree case whose associated quaternionic Shimura variety collapses to a zero-dimensional Shimura set, and it is cited as the case where the injectivity/doubling argument fails.
It is used as the critical middle case d=2, |I|=d/2 where the transfer argument/injectivity fails, with the associated target becoming a quaternionic Shimura set.

The d=2, |I|=1 case is the middle boundary |I|=d/2 where the associated quaternionic Shimura variety collapses to a Shimura set and the transfer map becomes zero, so it is used as the bad obstruction to injectivity.

The d=2, |I|=1 Hilbert modular surface is used as the middle-degree case |I|=d/2 where the transfer/injectivity argument fails; structurally it is a critical boundary case rather than a sporadic or maximally symmetric object.
